claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex. He's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Since Alex is 17 years old, he's over 16, so I need to ask a few more questions to determine your eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or an equivalent full-time course) or approved training (such as an unpaid, employer-provided training course)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment. He left school and decided not to continue with his studies.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex is 17 and has left education or training, there may still be a way to qualify for Child Benefit for a limited period.

**Has Alex registered with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed forces. He's actually got himself a full-time job, so he's been working instead.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one more question to clarify Alex's situation:

**Is Alex working 24 hours or more per week in his job?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's working full-time at his job.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Based on the information you've provided, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

- **Alex is 17 years old**, so to continue receiving Child Benefit for a child over 16, they normally need to remain in approved education or training.
- Alex has **left education** and is not in any approved training.
- He has **not registered** with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would have allowed Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks).
- Most importantly, Alex is **working full-time (24+ hours per week)** and is no longer in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training.

I'm sorry I couldn't bring you better news. If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training), you may be able to make a new claim at that point.

Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means he is over 16. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit can normally continue if the child stays in approved education or training. However, Alex has left education and is not in any approved training. He has also not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would have allowed Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks). Additionally, Alex is working full-time (24 hours or more per week) and is no longer in approved education or training. According to the rules, Child Benefit stops if a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Alex is 17 years old, has left education, has not registered with a careers service or armed services, and is now working full-time (24+ hours per week). Under the rules, Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child over 16 works 24 hours or more per week and is not in approved education or training.
